Statistical Learning Project

Homework 1: Unsupervised Learning

2024-11-08

Libraries

library(dplyr)
## 
## Attaching package: 'dplyr'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     filter, lag
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union
library(GGally)
## Loading required package: ggplot2
## Registered S3 method overwritten by 'GGally':
##   method from   
##   +.gg   ggplot2
library(ggplot2)
library(factoextra)
## Welcome! Want to learn more? See two factoextra-related books at https://goo.gl/ve3WBa
library(cluster)
library(mclust)
## Package 'mclust' version 6.1.1
## Type 'citation("mclust")' for citing this R package in publications.
library(kernlab)
## 
## Attaching package: 'kernlab'
## The following object is masked from 'package:ggplot2':
## 
##     alpha
library(MASS)
## 
## Attaching package: 'MASS'
## The following object is masked from 'package:dplyr':
## 
##     select

Dataset and variable selection

We have decided to use the R-library NHANES(National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey) dataset for our project. It is a dataset that shows the health data of survey takers in the year intervals 2009-2010 and 2011-2012. This dataset has 10000 observations and 76 variables.

install.packages("NHANES")
## Installing package into 'C:/Users/silvi/AppData/Local/R/win-library/4.4'
## (as 'lib' is unspecified)
## package 'NHANES' successfully unpacked and MD5 sums checked
## 
## The downloaded binary packages are in
##  C:\Users\silvi\AppData\Local\Temp\RtmpQLwKPf\downloaded_packages
library(NHANES)
## Warning: package 'NHANES' was built under R version 4.4.3

If the previous code doesn’t work due to different version of RStudio, the following code should work. Please set your working directory to be the folder with all the documents of our homework.

NHANES <- read.csv("NHANES_data.csv")
data("NHANES")
head(NHANES)
## # A tibble: 6 × 76
##      ID SurveyYr Gender   Age AgeDecade AgeMonths Race1 Race3 Education   
##   <int> <fct>    <fct>  <int> <fct>         <int> <fct> <fct> <fct>       
## 1 51624 2009_10  male      34 " 30-39"        409 White <NA>  High School 
## 2 51624 2009_10  male      34 " 30-39"        409 White <NA>  High School 
## 3 51624 2009_10  male      34 " 30-39"        409 White <NA>  High School 
## 4 51625 2009_10  male       4 " 0-9"           49 Other <NA>  <NA>        
## 5 51630 2009_10  female    49 " 40-49"        596 White <NA>  Some College
## 6 51638 2009_10  male       9 " 0-9"          115 White <NA>  <NA>        
## # ℹ 67 more variables: MaritalStatus <fct>, HHIncome <fct>, HHIncomeMid <int>,
## #   Poverty <dbl>, HomeRooms <int>, HomeOwn <fct>, Work <fct>, Weight <dbl>,
## #   Length <dbl>, HeadCirc <dbl>, Height <dbl>, BMI <dbl>,
## #   BMICatUnder20yrs <fct>, BMI_WHO <fct>, Pulse <int>, BPSysAve <int>,
## #   BPDiaAve <int>, BPSys1 <int>, BPDia1 <int>, BPSys2 <int>, BPDia2 <int>,
## #   BPSys3 <int>, BPDia3 <int>, Testosterone <dbl>, DirectChol <dbl>,
## #   TotChol <dbl>, UrineVol1 <int>, UrineFlow1 <dbl>, UrineVol2 <int>, …

Looking at the first three observations’ ID, we see that there are some repetitions. Our first step will be to remove those repetitions. Then looking at the summary of the data, we can see that there are columns that have a lot of NA values and variables that are factors. For our purposes of using this data for unsupervised learning, we can remove those variables.

# choose only the unique observations 
unique_data <- NHANES %>% distinct(ID, .keep_all = TRUE)
summary(unique_data)
##        ID           SurveyYr       Gender          Age          AgeDecade   
##  Min.   :51624   2009_10:3568   female:3420   Min.   : 0.00    0-9   :1109  
##  1st Qu.:56568   2011_12:3211   male  :3359   1st Qu.:15.00    10-19 :1016  
##  Median :61615                                Median :34.00    30-39 : 895  
##  Mean   :61657                                Mean   :35.45    20-29 : 880  
##  3rd Qu.:66778                                3rd Qu.:54.00    40-49 : 845  
##  Max.   :71915                                Max.   :80.00   (Other):1785  
##                                                               NA's   : 249  
##    AgeMonths          Race1           Race3               Education   
##  Min.   :  0.0   Black   :1024   Asian   : 252   8th Grade     : 359  
##  1st Qu.:170.0   Hispanic: 495   Black   : 514   9 - 11th Grade: 624  
##  Median :388.0   Mexican : 859   Hispanic: 274   High School   : 993  
##  Mean   :404.4   White   :3783   Mexican : 390   Some College  :1423  
##  3rd Qu.:616.0   Other   : 618   White   :1667   College Grad  :1246  
##  Max.   :959.0                   Other   : 114   NA's          :2134  
##  NA's   :3230                    NA's    :3568                        
##       MaritalStatus         HHIncome     HHIncomeMid        Poverty     
##  Divorced    : 443   more 99999 :1303   Min.   :  2500   Min.   :0.000  
##  LivePartner : 374   75000-99999: 691   1st Qu.: 22500   1st Qu.:1.090  
##  Married     :2463   25000-34999: 689   Median : 50000   Median :2.310

##  Separated   : 135   45000-54999: 511   3rd Qu.: 87500   3rd Qu.:4.340  
##  Widowed     : 336   (Other)    :2392   Max.   :100000   Max.   :5.000  
##  NA's        :2129   NA's       : 585   NA's   :585      NA's   :518    
##    HomeRooms       HomeOwn             Work          Weight      
##  Min.   : 1.000   Own  :4149   Looking   : 210   Min.   :  2.80  
##  1st Qu.: 5.000   Rent :2419   NotWorking:1984   1st Qu.: 53.40  
##  Median : 6.000   Other: 167   Working   :2852   Median : 71.10

##  3rd Qu.: 7.000                                  3rd Qu.: 87.60  
##  Max.   :13.000                                  Max.   :230.70  
##  NA's   :48                                      NA's   :55      
##      Length          HeadCirc         Height           BMI

##  1st Qu.:  2.00   Yes : 964   Yes :2056   Smoker    :2056   1st Qu.:15.00  
##  Median : 24.00   NA's:4723   NA's:2125   NA's      :2125   Median :17.00  
##  Mean   : 71.94                                             Mean   :17.83  
##  3rd Qu.:104.00                                             3rd Qu.:19.00  
##  Max.   :364.00                                             Max.   :72.00  
##  NA's   :3049                                               NA's   :4807   
##  Marijuana   AgeFirstMarij   RegularMarij  AgeRegMarij    HardDrugs  
##  No  :1356   Min.   : 1.00   No  :2256    Min.   : 5.00   No  :2986  
##  Yes :1727   1st Qu.:15.00   Yes : 827    1st Qu.:15.00   Yes : 629  
##  NA's:3696   Median :16.00   NA's:3696    Median :17.00   NA's:3164  
##              Mean   :17.02                Mean   :17.68              
##              3rd Qu.:18.00                3rd Qu.:19.00              
##              Max.   :48.00                Max.   :52.00              
##              NA's   :5053                 NA's   :5952               
##  SexEver         SexAge      SexNumPartnLife   SexNumPartYear   SameSex    
##  No  : 157   Min.   : 9.00   Min.   :   0.00   Min.   : 0.000   No  :3357  
##  Yes :3458   1st Qu.:15.00   1st Qu.:   2.00   1st Qu.: 1.000   Yes : 258  
##  NA's:3164   Median :17.00   Median :   5.00   Median : 1.000   NA's:3164  
##              Mean   :17.41   Mean   :  14.74   Mean   : 1.344              
##              3rd Qu.:19.00   3rd Qu.:  12.00   3rd Qu.: 1.000              
##              Max.   :50.00   Max.   :2000.00   Max.   :69.000              
##              NA's   :3325    NA's   :3192      NA's   :3705                
##       SexOrientation  PregnantNow  
##  Bisexual    :  80   Yes    :  49  
##  Heterosexual:2876   No     :1037  
##  Homosexual  :  53   Unknown:  33  
##  NA's        :3770   NA's   :5660  
##                                    
##                                    
## 
# Choose which variables will be most useful
my_data = unique_data %>% dplyr::select("Gender", "Age", "HHIncomeMid", "Weight", "Height", "BMI",
                                 "Pulse", "BPSysAve", "BPDiaAve", "TotChol", 
                                 "UrineVol1", "UrineFlow1", "DaysPhysHlthBad", 
                                 "DaysMentHlthBad", "SleepHrsNight", "PhysActiveDays",
                                 "SexAge")

Dealing with NA values

# Find which rows have the most NA values
na_count <- rowSums(is.na(my_data))
unique_na_counts <- unique(na_count)
unique_na_counts # there are rows with missing from 0 - 14
##  [1]  2 11  1  5  0  7  3  6  4 13  9  8 14 12 10
# As some rows have a lot of missing data, it would be best if we remove them. 
# Hence any rows that have more than 6 missing values will be removed
new_data <- my_data[!(na_count %in% c(6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14)), ]
# We are left with 5559 observations, which is good.
summary(new_data) 
##     Gender          Age         HHIncomeMid         Weight      
##  female:2808   Min.   : 8.00   Min.   :  2500   Min.   : 21.70  
##  male  :2751   1st Qu.:23.00   1st Qu.: 22500   1st Qu.: 62.40  
##                Median :40.00   Median : 50000   Median : 76.00  
##                Mean   :41.07   Mean   : 54602   Mean   : 77.88  
##                3rd Qu.:57.00   3rd Qu.: 87500   3rd Qu.: 90.65  
##                Max.   :80.00   Max.   :100000   Max.   :230.70  
##                                NA's   :449      NA's   :28      
##      Height           BMI            Pulse           BPSysAve    
##  Min.   :119.1   Min.   :12.90   Min.   : 40.00   Min.   : 76.0  
##  1st Qu.:159.4   1st Qu.:22.88   1st Qu.: 64.00   1st Qu.:106.0  
##  Median :167.1   Median :26.77   Median : 72.00   Median :116.0  
##  Mean   :166.7   Mean   :27.76   Mean   : 73.44   Mean   :118.4  
##  3rd Qu.:175.0   3rd Qu.:31.50   3rd Qu.: 82.00   3rd Qu.:127.0  
##  Max.   :200.4   Max.   :81.25   Max.   :136.00   Max.   :226.0  
##  NA's   :27      NA's   :32      NA's   :76       NA's   :84     
##     BPDiaAve         TotChol         UrineVol1       UrineFlow1     
##  Min.   :  0.00   Min.   : 1.530   Min.   :  0.0   Min.   : 0.0000  
##  1st Qu.: 60.00   1st Qu.: 4.110   1st Qu.: 50.0   1st Qu.: 0.4040  
##  Median : 68.00   Median : 4.780   Median : 94.0   Median : 0.6920  
##  Mean   : 67.05   Mean   : 4.872   Mean   :119.2   Mean   : 0.9662  
##  3rd Qu.: 76.00   3rd Qu.: 5.530   3rd Qu.:165.0   3rd Qu.: 1.2140  
##  Max.   :116.00   Max.   :13.650   Max.   :510.0   Max.   :12.3460  
##  NA's   :84       NA's   :306      NA's   :38      NA's   :346      
##  DaysPhysHlthBad  DaysMentHlthBad  SleepHrsNight    PhysActiveDays 
##  Min.   : 0.000   Min.   : 0.000   Min.   : 2.000   Min.   :1.000  
##  1st Qu.: 0.000   1st Qu.: 0.000   1st Qu.: 6.000   1st Qu.:2.000  
##  Median : 0.000   Median : 0.000   Median : 7.000   Median :3.000  
##  Mean   : 3.497   Mean   : 4.114   Mean   : 6.916   Mean   :3.736  
##  3rd Qu.: 3.000   3rd Qu.: 4.000   3rd Qu.: 8.000   3rd Qu.:5.000  
##  Max.   :30.000   Max.   :30.000   Max.   :12.000   Max.   :7.000  
##  NA's   :692      NA's   :692      NA's   :699      NA's   :2790   
##      SexAge     
##  Min.   : 9.00  
##  1st Qu.:15.00  
##  Median :17.00  
##  Mean   :17.41  
##  3rd Qu.:19.00  
##  Max.   :50.00  
##  NA's   :2112
# With the summary we can see that every column has a lot of missing values, 
# but PhysActiveDays and SexAge have the most(>2100), so it would be better 
# to remove those columns.
new_data = new_data %>% dplyr::select(-PhysActiveDays, -SexAge)

For the rest of the variables we will replace the NA values with the average of each variable.

# As BMI is calculated based on weight and height, we can replace the the NA 
# values in that column by calculating it. 
new_data$Weight[is.na(new_data$Weight)] <- mean(new_data$Weight, na.rm = TRUE)
new_data$Height[is.na(new_data$Height)] <- mean(new_data$Height, na.rm = TRUE)
new_data$BMI[is.na(new_data$BMI)] <- new_data$Weight[is.na(new_data$BMI)] / (new_data$Height[is.na(new_data$BMI)]^2)
new_data <- new_data %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ ifelse(is.na(.), mean(., na.rm = TRUE), .)))

As that function will make all of the integer values into numerical, we will transform them back to integers.

new_data$Pulse = as.integer(new_data$Pulse)
new_data$BPSysAve = as.integer(new_data$BPSysAve)
new_data$BPDiaAve = as.integer(new_data$BPDiaAve)
new_data$UrineVol1 = as.integer(new_data$UrineVol1)
new_data$DaysPhysHlthBad = as.integer(new_data$DaysPhysHlthBad)
new_data$DaysMentHlthBad = as.integer(new_data$DaysMentHlthBad)
new_data$SleepHrsNight = as.integer(new_data$SleepHrsNight)
sum(rowSums(is.na(new_data)))  # check if there are any NA values now
## [1] 0

Feature engineering.

Creating new variables that may be of use for us.

# Any abnormal BPRatio may indicate cardiac issues 
# As there are some observations with BPDiaAve = 0, which would not work for us,
# we have decided to remove those records. 
new_data <- new_data %>% filter(BPDiaAve != 0)
new_data$BPRatio <- new_data$BPSysAve / new_data$BPDiaAve

# Total Bad Health Days will show us how many days of the year the person didn't feel good.
new_data$TotalBadHealthDays <- new_data$DaysPhysHlthBad + new_data$DaysMentHlthBad

# SleepAdjusted shows if based on age, a person is sleeping enough. We will remove
# SleepHrsNight and replace it with this variable as it would give a more accurate 
# interpretation if the person sleeps enough for their age.
new_data$SleepAdjusted <- ifelse(new_data$Age < 18, new_data$SleepHrsNight / 9, 
                           ifelse(new_data$Age < 65, new_data$SleepHrsNight / 7, new_data$SleepHrsNight / 7))
new_data = new_data %>% dplyr::select(-SleepHrsNight)

# Cholesterol BMI Ration shows the relation between the cholesterol levels and body mass.
new_data$CholesterolBMIRatio <- new_data$TotChol/new_data$BMI

# PulseAgeRatio can indicate health problems if the ratio is abnormal for the age 
new_data$PulseAgeRatio <- new_data$Pulse/new_data$Age

Normalization of variables:

normalize_z <- function(x) {(x - mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)}
normalized_new_data <- new_data %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), normalize_z))
numerical_data = normalized_new_data %>% dplyr::select(where(is.numeric))
summary(numerical_data)
##       Age            HHIncomeMid          Weight             Height        
##  Min.   :-1.62066   Min.   :-1.6509   Min.   :-2.46423   Min.   :-4.16012  
##  1st Qu.:-0.86298   1st Qu.:-0.7802   1st Qu.:-0.67572   1st Qu.:-0.63109  
##  Median :-0.05641   Median :-0.1471   Median :-0.07663   Median : 0.02243  
##  Mean   : 0.00000   Mean   : 0.0000   Mean   : 0.00000   Mean   : 0.00000  
##  3rd Qu.: 0.77460   3rd Qu.: 1.0402   3rd Qu.: 0.55087   3rd Qu.: 0.71081  
##  Max.   : 1.89890   Max.   : 1.4359   Max.   : 6.67509   Max.   : 2.92407  
##       BMI              Pulse            BPSysAve          BPDiaAve       
##  Min.   :-3.7615   Min.   :-2.7712   Min.   :-2.4445   Min.   :-4.35296  
##  1st Qu.:-0.6533   1st Qu.:-0.7782   1st Qu.:-0.7143   1st Qu.:-0.51340  
##  Median :-0.1196   Median :-0.1139   Median :-0.1375   Median : 0.02414  
##  Mean   : 0.0000   Mean   : 0.0000   Mean   : 0.0000   Mean   : 0.00000  
##  3rd Qu.: 0.5189   3rd Qu.: 0.5504   3rd Qu.: 0.4969   3rd Qu.: 0.63847  
##  Max.   : 7.3006   Max.   : 5.2007   Max.   : 6.2068   Max.   : 3.71011  
##     TotChol           UrineVol1         UrineFlow1      DaysPhysHlthBad   
##  Min.   :-3.19569   Min.   :-1.3189   Min.   :-1.0797   Min.   :-0.48219  
##  1st Qu.:-0.70285   1st Qu.:-0.7661   1st Qu.:-0.6100   1st Qu.:-0.48219  
##  Median :-0.01517   Median :-0.2796   Median :-0.2430   Median :-0.48219  
##  Mean   : 0.00000   Mean   : 0.0000   Mean   : 0.0000   Mean   : 0.00000  
##  3rd Qu.: 0.57699   3rd Qu.: 0.5054   3rd Qu.: 0.2188   3rd Qu.:-0.06078  
##  Max.   : 8.38024   Max.   : 4.3198   Max.   :12.6925   Max.   : 3.73184  
##  DaysMentHlthBad       BPRatio        TotalBadHealthDays SleepAdjusted    
##  Min.   :-0.55648   Min.   :-1.3158   Min.   :-0.65811   Min.   :-3.1108  
##  1st Qu.:-0.55648   1st Qu.:-0.5052   1st Qu.:-0.65811   1st Qu.:-0.5856  
##  Median :-0.42067   Median :-0.2109   Median :-0.39592   Median : 0.2812  
##  Mean   : 0.00000   Mean   : 0.0000   Mean   : 0.00000   Mean   : 0.0000  
##  3rd Qu.:-0.01324   3rd Qu.: 0.1763   3rd Qu.:-0.04634   3rd Qu.: 0.9596  
##  Max.   : 3.51780   Max.   :14.4550   Max.   : 4.58565   Max.   : 3.6732  
##  CholesterolBMIRatio PulseAgeRatio    
##  Min.   :-0.07108    Min.   :-1.0248  
##  1st Qu.:-0.07039    1st Qu.:-0.6732  
##  Median :-0.07017    Median :-0.3641  
##  Mean   : 0.00000    Mean   : 0.0000  
##  3rd Qu.:-0.06992    3rd Qu.: 0.2963  
##  Max.   :32.36205    Max.   : 6.1583

Dealing with outliers:

boxplot(numerical_data[, sapply(numerical_data, is.numeric)],las = 2, cex.axis = 0.5)

# 1) Identify them
identify_outliers <- function(x) {
  Q1 <- quantile(x, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE)
  Q3 <- quantile(x, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
  IQR_value <- Q3 - Q1
  outliers <- x < (Q1 - 1.5 * IQR_value) | x > (Q3 + 1.5 * IQR_value)
  return(outliers)
}

# Apply outlier function to each numeric column
outlier_matrix <- sapply(numerical_data[, sapply(numerical_data, is.numeric)], identify_outliers)

# Check which columns and rows have outliers
outliers_by_variable <- colSums(outlier_matrix) # Number of outliers per variable
outliers_by_variable
##                 Age         HHIncomeMid              Weight              Height 
##                   0                   0                 109                  88 
##                 BMI               Pulse            BPSysAve            BPDiaAve 
##                 177                  51                 155                 175 
##             TotChol           UrineVol1          UrineFlow1     DaysPhysHlthBad 
##                  97                 174                 374                 626 
##     DaysMentHlthBad             BPRatio  TotalBadHealthDays       SleepAdjusted 
##                 736                 311                 754                  24 
## CholesterolBMIRatio       PulseAgeRatio 
##                 108                 395

From that we can see that we have many outliers in the columns DaysPhysHlthBad, DaysMentHlthBad, and TotalBadHealthDays. From this we can judge that they have high uniqueness relative to the rest of the variables, which can mess with our models, so it would be best to remove these columns and do the unsupervised learning on the rest of the variables.

numerical_data = numerical_data %>% dplyr::select(-DaysPhysHlthBad, -DaysMentHlthBad, 
                                           -TotalBadHealthDays)

Before removing the outliers, we can check the correlation matrix to see if we can remove some columns that don’t show any strong relationships or some that have only one strong relation.

ggcorr(numerical_data)

From this we can see that CholestrolBMIRatio only has weak relations, UrineVol1 and UrineFlow1 are strongly corelated, so we can remove one of them (remove UrineFlow1 as it has more outliers). We decided to keep both BMI and Weight for now.

numerical_data = numerical_data %>% dplyr::select(-CholesterolBMIRatio, -UrineFlow1)
  1. Remove rows with outliers
# new outlier matrix
outlier_matrix <- sapply(numerical_data[, sapply(numerical_data, is.numeric)], identify_outliers)
cleaned_data <- numerical_data[!rowSums(outlier_matrix), ]

Checking the standard deviation

sd_values = sapply(cleaned_data[, sapply(cleaned_data, is.numeric)], sd, na.rm = TRUE)
sd_df <- data.frame(Variable = names(sd_values), SD = sd_values)
ggplot(sd_df, aes(x = reorder(Variable, -SD), y = SD)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "pink") +
  coord_flip() +  # Flip the chart for easier readability
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Standard Deviation of Each Variable", x = "Variable", y = "Standard Deviation")

From this we can see that BPRatio has a relatively smaller standard deviation, which can mean that it does not contribute much to the variability of the data. Hence, we can remove it.

numerical_data = numerical_data %>% dplyr::select(-BPRatio)
outlier_matrix <- sapply(numerical_data[, sapply(numerical_data, is.numeric)], identify_outliers)
final_cleaned_data <- numerical_data[!rowSums(outlier_matrix), ]
boxplot(final_cleaned_data[, sapply(final_cleaned_data, is.numeric)],las = 2, cex.axis = 0.5)

# Visualization

  1. Create histograms to show the distribution of each variable.

This allows us to see which variables are normally distributed, which are skewed, which are multimodal(have peaks in the histogram), or which have low variance.

numeric_vars <- c("Age", "HHIncomeMid", "Weight", "Height", "BMI", "Pulse", "BPSysAve", 
                  "BPDiaAve", "TotChol", "UrineVol1", "SleepAdjusted", "PulseAgeRatio")
for (var in numeric_vars) {
  p <- ggplot(final_cleaned_data, aes_string(var)) +
    geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "pink", color = "red") +
    theme_minimal() +
    ggtitle(paste("Distribution of", var)) +
    xlab(var) + ylab("Frequency") +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))
  
  print(p)
}
## Warning: `aes_string()` was deprecated in ggplot2 3.0.0.
## ℹ Please use tidy evaluation idioms with `aes()`.
## ℹ See also `vignette("ggplot2-in-packages")` for more information.
## This warning is displayed once every 8 hours.
## Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was
## generated.

If we were to use UrineVol1 or PulseAgeRatio for clustering, we will need to transform them as they are skewed. SleepAdjusted has some peaks, which suggest that it already has some natural clusters.

Deal with skewness.

Only UrineVol1 and PulseAgeRatio really exhibits heavy skewness.

# for UrineVol1
offset <- abs(min(final_cleaned_data$UrineVol1)) + 1  
boxcox_result <- boxcox((final_cleaned_data$UrineVol1 + offset) ~ 1)

lambda <- boxcox_result$x[which.max(boxcox_result$y)]
final_cleaned_data$UrineVol1 <- ((final_cleaned_data$UrineVol1 + offset)^lambda - 1) / lambda

p <- ggplot(final_cleaned_data, aes_string("UrineVol1")) +
    geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "pink", color = "red") +
    theme_minimal() +
    ggtitle(paste("Distribution of", "UrineVol1")) +
    xlab("UrineVol1") + ylab("Frequency") +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))
  
print(p)

#for PulseAgeRatio
offset <- abs(min(final_cleaned_data$PulseAgeRatio)) + 1  
boxcox_result <- boxcox((final_cleaned_data$PulseAgeRatio + offset) ~ 1)

lambda <- boxcox_result$x[which.max(boxcox_result$y)]
final_cleaned_data$PulseAgeRatio <- ((final_cleaned_data$PulseAgeRatio + offset)^lambda - 1) / lambda

p <- ggplot(final_cleaned_data, aes_string("PulseAgeRatio")) +
    geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "pink", color = "red") +
    theme_minimal() +
    ggtitle(paste("Distribution of", "PulseAgeRatio")) +
    xlab("PulseAgeRatio") + ylab("Frequency") +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))
  
print(p)

Correlation

Correlation of the numerical data we have decided to keep:

ggcorr(final_cleaned_data)

From this graph we can see that there aren’t a lot of variables that have a high correlation between each other. Weight and Height have a strong positive relation as well as BPSysAve and BPDiaAve. Weight and BMI have a moderately strong positive relation. Variables like UrineVol1 and SleepAdjusted very weak relations with the rest. Also, PulseAgeRation shows negative relations with some of the variables.

PCA

pca = prcomp(final_cleaned_data, scale=T)
summary(pca) 
## Importance of components:
##                           PC1    PC2    PC3     PC4     PC5     PC6     PC7
## Standard deviation     1.7008 1.3529 1.1627 1.09528 1.00535 0.95920 0.94189
## Proportion of Variance 0.2411 0.1525 0.1126 0.09997 0.08423 0.07667 0.07393
## Cumulative Proportion  0.2411 0.3936 0.5062 0.60621 0.69044 0.76711 0.84104
##                            PC8     PC9    PC10    PC11    PC12
## Standard deviation     0.86612 0.81172 0.69350 0.11158 0.07129
## Proportion of Variance 0.06251 0.05491 0.04008 0.00104 0.00042
## Cumulative Proportion  0.90355 0.95846 0.99854 0.99958 1.00000

We can observe that with 7 variables we are able to explain 84.1% of the data.

screeplot(pca,main="Screeplot",col="pink",type="barplot",pch=19) #Variable importance

A nicer view than screeplot is Elbow Method (factoextra)

fviz_screeplot(pca, addlabels = TRUE)

We can reaffirm with this graph that PCA can be developed correctly with 7 variables

Let us go component by component

First component:

barplot(pca$rotation[,1], las=2, col="pink")

# This barplot shows all the values in the first column (var 1) of the vector matrix

sum(pca$rotation[,1]^2) #This sum should always be equal to 1
## [1] 1
fviz_contrib(pca, choice = "var", axes = 1)

We can observe that the most relevant variables for the first component are: PulseAgeRatio, Age, BPSysAve, Weight and BMI

The first component appears to represent a general health index, particularly cardiovascular health and factors related to age and body weight.

Top 10 people in general health index:

# use the data before normalization to get the real values
normal_cleaned_data = new_data %>% dplyr::select(-DaysPhysHlthBad, -DaysMentHlthBad, 
                                      -TotalBadHealthDays, -CholesterolBMIRatio,
                                      -UrineFlow1, -BPRatio)
normal_cleaned_data <- normal_cleaned_data[!rowSums(outlier_matrix), ]

# Get the indices of the top 10 observations along the first principal component
top_indices1 <- order(pca$x[,1], decreasing = TRUE)[1:10]

# Use these indices to subset the original dataset and view the variable information
top_observations1 <- normal_cleaned_data[top_indices1, ]

# Display the top observations
print(top_observations1)
## # A tibble: 10 × 13
##    Gender   Age HHIncomeMid Weight Height   BMI Pulse BPSysAve BPDiaAve TotChol
##    <fct>  <int>       <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> <int>    <int>    <int>   <dbl>
##  1 male      13      100000   29.1   148.  13.3    72       94       40    4.55
##  2 male      11       30000   31.8   143.  15.6    62       97       41    3.47
##  3 female     9       87500   28.7   140.  14.8    54       93       47    4.53
##  4 female    16       40000   49.1   155.  20.5    92       96       39    3.98
##  5 female    10       87500   26.6   140   13.6    58      103       59    4.5 
##  6 female    13       40000   39.4   149.  17.7    76       90       53    4.87
##  7 male      15      100000   42.6   171.  14.6    82       87       61    3.98
##  8 male      11       40000   38.2   147.  17.6    64      103       50    3.8 
##  9 male      12      100000   39.6   155.  16.4    66       90       64    3.8 
## 10 female    14       22500   45.3   150.  20      80       92       60    3.47
## # ℹ 3 more variables: UrineVol1 <int>, SleepAdjusted <dbl>, PulseAgeRatio <dbl>

Second component:

barplot(pca$rotation[,2], las=2, col="pink")

barplot(abs(pca$rotation[,2]), las=2, col="pink")

fviz_contrib(pca, choice = "var", axes = 2)

We can observe that the most relevant variables for the second component are: Weight, Height, BMI, Age and PulseAgeRatio

The second component seems to capture a body composition index, focusing on characteristics that define physical development, body size, and possibly the influence of age on these metrics.

Third component:

barplot(pca$rotation[,3], las=2, col="pink")

barplot(abs(pca$rotation[,3]), las=2, col="pink")

fviz_contrib(pca, choice = "var", axes = 3)

We can observe that the most relevant variables for the third component are: Pulse, HHIncomeMide, Height, BMI and UrineVol1

The third component seems to represent a socioeconomic and physiological health index.

Top 10 people in socioeconomic and physiological health index:

# Get the indices of the top 10 observations along the first principal component
top_indices2 <- order(pca$x[,2], decreasing = TRUE)[1:10]

# Use these indices to subset the original dataset and view the variable information
top_observations2 <- normal_cleaned_data[top_indices2, ]

# Display the top observations
print(top_observations2)
## # A tibble: 10 × 13
##    Gender   Age HHIncomeMid Weight Height   BMI Pulse BPSysAve BPDiaAve TotChol
##    <fct>  <int>       <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> <int>    <int>    <int>   <dbl>
##  1 female    80       30000   48.5   144.  23.3    56      141       45    6.7 
##  2 female    80       12500   42.5   146.  19.8    66      158       52    5.12
##  3 female    80      100000   40.2   148.  18.3    52      148       64    6.18
##  4 female    80       22500   43.5   156.  17.8    64      104       46    5.02
##  5 female    80       40000   45.6   163.  17.2    50      122       48    4.45
##  6 female    80       22500   46.3   156.  18.9    56      143       61    6.49
##  7 female    69        7500   37     156.  15.2    74      111       47    5.74
##  8 female    79       30000   51.3   157.  20.9    56      140       63    4.01
##  9 female    70        7500   46.2   149.  20.9    62      153       71    5.09
## 10 female    80      100000   45.3   148   20.7    66      146       70    5.61
## # ℹ 3 more variables: UrineVol1 <int>, SleepAdjusted <dbl>, PulseAgeRatio <dbl>

Fourth component:

barplot(pca$rotation[,4], las=2, col="pink")

barplot(abs(pca$rotation[,4]), las=2, col="pink")

fviz_contrib(pca, choice = "var", axes = 4)

We can observe that the most relevant variables for the fourth component are: BPDiaAVe, Pulse, TotChol and BMI

The fourth component appears to represent a cardiovascular and metabolic health risk index.

Fifth component:

barplot(pca$rotation[,5], las=2, col="pink")

barplot(abs(pca$rotation[,5]), las=2, col="pink")

fviz_contrib(pca, choice = "var", axes = 5)

We can observe that the most relevant variables for the fifth component are: SleepAdjusted, HHIncomeMid and BPSysAve

The fifth component seems to represent a lifestyle and socioeconomic health index.

Sixth component:

barplot(pca$rotation[,6], las=2, col="pink")

barplot(abs(pca$rotation[,6]), las=2, col="pink")

fviz_contrib(pca, choice = "var", axes = 6)

We can observe that the most relevant variables for the sixth component are: UrineVol1, SleepAdjusted and HHIncomeMid

PC6 may represent a component associated with lifestyle and health factors influenced by socioeconomic status, sleep patterns, and physical health indicators.

Seventh component:

barplot(pca$rotation[,7], las=2, col="pink")

barplot(abs(pca$rotation[,7]), las=2, col="pink")

fviz_contrib(pca, choice = "var", axes = 7)

We can observe that the most relevant variables for the seventh component are: TotChol, UrineVol1, Height, BPSysAve and SleepAdjusted

PC7 might capture aspects of cardiovascular and metabolic health, influenced by both physiological markers (like cholesterol and blood pressure) and lifestyle elements (like sleep). This component could help differentiate individuals based on combined cardiovascular risk factors and body-related attributes.

Factor analysis

The more factors that we have the better the variance explained will be, but this can lead to overfitting. Hence, we need to find the balance between the two.

# Factor analysis with all numeric data(should limit them)
fa_result <- factanal(final_cleaned_data, factors = 4, rotation="varimax", scores="regression", lower = 0.01)
fa_result
## 
## Call:
## factanal(x = final_cleaned_data, factors = 4, scores = "regression",     rotation = "varimax", lower = 0.01)
## 
## Uniquenesses:
##           Age   HHIncomeMid        Weight        Height           BMI 
##         0.023         0.965         0.010         0.010         0.010 
##         Pulse      BPSysAve      BPDiaAve       TotChol     UrineVol1 
##         0.010         0.775         0.916         0.912         0.962 
## SleepAdjusted PulseAgeRatio 
##         0.955         0.010 
## 
## Loadings:
##               Factor1 Factor2 Factor3 Factor4
## Age            0.984                         
## HHIncomeMid                    0.176         
## Weight         0.148   0.924   0.335         
## Height                 0.167   0.974   0.120 
## BMI            0.178   0.963  -0.180         
## Pulse                         -0.220   0.965 
## BPSysAve       0.446   0.151                 
## BPDiaAve       0.168   0.157   0.108   0.139 
## TotChol        0.280                         
## UrineVol1                      0.167         
## SleepAdjusted  0.210                         
## PulseAgeRatio -0.943                   0.313 
## 
##                Factor1 Factor2 Factor3 Factor4
## SS loadings      2.274   1.871   1.225   1.076
## Proportion Var   0.190   0.156   0.102   0.090
## Cumulative Var   0.190   0.345   0.448   0.537
## 
## Test of the hypothesis that 4 factors are sufficient.
## The chi square statistic is 2610.82 on 24 degrees of freedom.
## The p-value is 0

For using all the variables available, we can see that the best solution will be using four factors as we will get a relatively good variance explained and not be in great risk of overfitting. However, as we can see by the chi square statistics, this model does not have the best fit. Changing the rotation or the scores here doesn’t really affect the model. We can now try and remove some of the variables that are not well explained by the model. To do that we need to check the uniqueness of this factor analysis.

fa_result$uniquenesses
##           Age   HHIncomeMid        Weight        Height           BMI 
##    0.02313887    0.96487502    0.01000000    0.01000000    0.01000000 
##         Pulse      BPSysAve      BPDiaAve       TotChol     UrineVol1 
##    0.01000000    0.77499685    0.91596888    0.91240573    0.96170938 
## SleepAdjusted PulseAgeRatio 
##    0.95476077    0.01000000

Judging by the uniqueness of the variables, we can see that HHIncomeMid, TotChol, UrineVol1 and SleepAdjusted all have above 0.91 uniqueness, which means that more 91% of the variance in these variable cannot be explained by the model. As we cannot find better models by changing the factors, rotation or scores, we can remove those variables.

final_cleaned_data1 = final_cleaned_data %>% dplyr::select(-HHIncomeMid, -TotChol,
                                                    -UrineVol1, -SleepAdjusted) 
fa_result_1_f4 <- factanal(final_cleaned_data1, factors = 4, rotation="promax", scores="regression",
                        lower = 0.01)
fa_result_1_f4
## 
## Call:
## factanal(x = final_cleaned_data1, factors = 4, scores = "regression",     rotation = "promax", lower = 0.01)
## 
## Uniquenesses:
##           Age        Weight        Height           BMI         Pulse 
##         0.010         0.010         0.010         0.010         0.010 
##      BPSysAve      BPDiaAve PulseAgeRatio 
##         0.769         0.922         0.021 
## 
## Loadings:
##               Factor1 Factor2 Factor3 Factor4
## Age            1.024                   0.109 
## Weight                 0.881   0.293         
## Height                         1.011         
## BMI                    1.047  -0.250         
## Pulse                                  0.987 
## BPSysAve       0.442                         
## BPDiaAve       0.129   0.113   0.135   0.138 
## PulseAgeRatio -0.944                   0.171 
## 
##                Factor1 Factor2 Factor3 Factor4
## SS loadings      2.156   1.902   1.194   1.038
## Proportion Var   0.270   0.238   0.149   0.130
## Cumulative Var   0.270   0.507   0.656   0.786
## 
## Factor Correlations:
##         Factor1 Factor2 Factor3 Factor4
## Factor1  1.0000 -0.2759 -0.2923 -0.0715
## Factor2 -0.2759  1.0000  0.0366 -0.2040
## Factor3 -0.2923  0.0366  1.0000 -0.0540
## Factor4 -0.0715 -0.2040 -0.0540  1.0000
## 
## Test of the hypothesis that 4 factors are sufficient.
## The chi square statistic is 2038.64 on 2 degrees of freedom.
## The p-value is 0
fa_result_1_f3 <- factanal(final_cleaned_data1, factors = 3, rotation="promax", scores="regression",
                        lower = 0.01)
fa_result_1_f3
## 
## Call:
## factanal(x = final_cleaned_data1, factors = 3, scores = "regression",     rotation = "promax", lower = 0.01)
## 
## Uniquenesses:
##           Age        Weight        Height           BMI         Pulse 
##         0.093         0.010         0.010         0.010         0.828 
##      BPSysAve      BPDiaAve PulseAgeRatio 
##         0.791         0.939         0.010 
## 
## Loadings:
##               Factor1 Factor2 Factor3
## Age           -0.953                 
## Weight                 0.881   0.298 
## Height                         1.005 
## BMI                    1.028  -0.240 
## Pulse          0.402   0.159  -0.128 
## BPSysAve      -0.402   0.127         
## BPDiaAve      -0.104   0.139   0.117 
## PulseAgeRatio  0.998                 
## 
##                Factor1 Factor2 Factor3
## SS loadings      2.245   1.895   1.195
## Proportion Var   0.281   0.237   0.149
## Cumulative Var   0.281   0.517   0.667
## 
## Factor Correlations:
##         Factor1 Factor2 Factor3
## Factor1   1.000 -0.2614 -0.2247
## Factor2  -0.261  1.0000  0.0765
## Factor3  -0.225  0.0765  1.0000
## 
## Test of the hypothesis that 3 factors are sufficient.
## The chi square statistic is 6927.09 on 7 degrees of freedom.
## The p-value is 0

For this variables, the best model would be with four factors as well as it explains a lot of the data(78.6%) and the risk of overfitting is not very high as its complexity is not very high. Models will less factors will be less prone to overfitting, but their complexity will also decline a lot. The fit is still bad so we can try to remove other variables and see if we can find a better model. Based on uniqueness we can try to remove Pulse as well.

final_cleaned_data2 = final_cleaned_data1 %>% dplyr::select(-Pulse)
fa_result_2_f2 <- factanal(final_cleaned_data2, factors = 2, rotation="varimax", scores="Bartlett", lower = 0.01)
fa_result_2_f2
## 
## Call:
## factanal(x = final_cleaned_data2, factors = 2, scores = "Bartlett",     rotation = "varimax", lower = 0.01)
## 
## Uniquenesses:
##           Age        Weight        Height           BMI      BPSysAve 
##         0.943         0.010         0.010         0.010         0.947 
##      BPDiaAve PulseAgeRatio 
##         0.951         0.952 
## 
## Loadings:
##               Factor1 Factor2
## Age            0.235         
## Weight         0.777   0.623 
## Height        -0.162   0.982 
## BMI            0.985   0.146 
## BPSysAve       0.205   0.105 
## BPDiaAve       0.139   0.172 
## PulseAgeRatio -0.219         
## 
##                Factor1 Factor2
## SS loadings      1.765   1.416
## Proportion Var   0.252   0.202
## Cumulative Var   0.252   0.455
## 
## Test of the hypothesis that 2 factors are sufficient.
## The chi square statistic is 12171.82 on 8 degrees of freedom.
## The p-value is 0
fa_result_2_f3 <- factanal(final_cleaned_data2, factors = 3, rotation="varimax", scores="Bartlett", lower = 0.01)
fa_result_2_f3
## 
## Call:
## factanal(x = final_cleaned_data2, factors = 3, scores = "Bartlett",     rotation = "varimax", lower = 0.01)
## 
## Uniquenesses:
##           Age        Weight        Height           BMI      BPSysAve 
##         0.010         0.010         0.010         0.010         0.766 
##      BPDiaAve PulseAgeRatio 
##         0.941         0.092 
## 
## Loadings:
##               Factor1 Factor2 Factor3
## Age            0.991                 
## Weight         0.109   0.904   0.404 
## Height                         0.990 
## BMI            0.146   0.979  -0.110 
## BPSysAve       0.450   0.161         
## BPDiaAve       0.118   0.163   0.136 
## PulseAgeRatio -0.949                 
## 
##                Factor1 Factor2 Factor3
## SS loadings      2.134   1.851   1.181
## Proportion Var   0.305   0.264   0.169
## Cumulative Var   0.305   0.569   0.738
## 
## Test of the hypothesis that 3 factors are sufficient.
## The chi square statistic is 1421.28 on 3 degrees of freedom.
## The p-value is 7.1e-308

The model with 2 factors will explain less than 50% and based on the uniqueness, we can see that age, BPSysAve and BPDiaAve are not explained almost at all. For these variables the best model is with 3 factors as it explains a lot of the variance(73.8%) and it has only three factors, which means that the risk of overfitting is not very high. This is our best model for this data as the chi square statistics is very high. Based on uniqueness we can try to remove BPDiaAve as well.

final_cleaned_data3 = final_cleaned_data2 %>% dplyr::select(-BPDiaAve)
fa_result_3 <- factanal(final_cleaned_data3, factors = 3, rotation="varimax", scores="Bartlett", lower = 0.01)
fa_result_3
## 
## Call:
## factanal(x = final_cleaned_data3, factors = 3, scores = "Bartlett",     rotation = "varimax", lower = 0.01)
## 
## Uniquenesses:
##           Age        Weight        Height           BMI      BPSysAve 
##         0.010         0.010         0.010         0.010         0.766 
## PulseAgeRatio 
##         0.092 
## 
## Loadings:
##               Factor1 Factor2 Factor3
## Age            0.990                 
## Weight         0.134   0.901   0.405 
## Height                         0.991 
## BMI            0.160   0.977  -0.110 
## BPSysAve       0.454   0.153         
## PulseAgeRatio -0.950                 
## 
##                Factor1 Factor2 Factor3
## SS loadings      2.134   1.806   1.166
## Proportion Var   0.356   0.301   0.194
## Cumulative Var   0.356   0.657   0.851
## 
## The degrees of freedom for the model is 0 and the fit was 0.1838

The best model here will be again using 3 factors as it will explain 85.1% of the data, and we finally have a model that has a good fit. Using two factors will not capture enough of the underlining patterns and be a worse model. We can try removing BPSysAve instead of BPDiaAve now.

final_cleaned_data4 = final_cleaned_data1 %>% dplyr::select(-Pulse, -BPSysAve)
fa_result_4 <- factanal(final_cleaned_data4, factors = 3, rotation="varimax", scores="Bartlett", lower = 0.01)
fa_result_4
## 
## Call:
## factanal(x = final_cleaned_data4, factors = 3, scores = "Bartlett",     rotation = "varimax", lower = 0.01)
## 
## Uniquenesses:
##           Age        Weight        Height           BMI      BPDiaAve 
##         0.091         0.010         0.010         0.010         0.939 
## PulseAgeRatio 
##         0.010 
## 
## Loadings:
##               Factor1 Factor2 Factor3
## Age            0.946   0.116         
## Weight                 0.907   0.406 
## Height                         0.989 
## BMI            0.114   0.984  -0.107 
## BPDiaAve       0.119   0.165   0.138 
## PulseAgeRatio -0.988  -0.110         
## 
##                Factor1 Factor2 Factor3
## SS loadings      1.906   1.853   1.175
## Proportion Var   0.318   0.309   0.196
## Cumulative Var   0.318   0.626   0.822
## 
## The degrees of freedom for the model is 0 and the fit was 0.1805

Here we get a little worse fit than the previous one judjing by the cumulative variance and fit. Using two factors will not capture enough of the underlining patterns(variance 52%) and be a worse model. Lastly, we check without Pulse and PulseAgeRatio:

final_cleaned_data5 = final_cleaned_data1 %>% dplyr::select(-Pulse, -PulseAgeRatio)
fa_result_5 <- factanal(final_cleaned_data5, factors = 3, rotation="varimax", scores="Bartlett", lower = 0.01)
fa_result_5
## 
## Call:
## factanal(x = final_cleaned_data5, factors = 3, scores = "Bartlett",     rotation = "varimax", lower = 0.01)
## 
## Uniquenesses:
##      Age   Weight   Height      BMI BPSysAve BPDiaAve 
##    0.762    0.010    0.010    0.010    0.010    0.825 
## 
## Loadings:
##          Factor1 Factor2 Factor3
## Age       0.130   0.459  -0.101 
## Weight    0.893   0.178   0.403 
## Height                    0.991 
## BMI       0.971   0.189  -0.111 
## BPSysAve          0.994         
## BPDiaAve  0.106   0.384   0.127 
## 
##                Factor1 Factor2 Factor3
## SS loadings      1.780   1.413   1.185
## Proportion Var   0.297   0.236   0.197
## Cumulative Var   0.297   0.532   0.730
## 
## The degrees of freedom for the model is 0 and the fit was 0.1835

This model is fitting okay, but it has two variables with high uniqueness.

Choosing the best model and explaining its loadings:

Best model overall would be fa_result_3 as it explains the most variance 85.1% and has the lowest uniqueness, which means that it captures the underlining patterns the best. However, it possesses the risk of overfitting.

The best model for predicting new data would be model fa_result_2_f3 as it is a good balance between variance (73.8%), fit, and complexity. The risk of overfitting is less than with fa_result_3, but it still explains more than fa_result and fa_result_1_f4.

For fa_result_3(best overall model) we have the following uniqueness and loadings:

fa_result_3$uniquenesses
##           Age        Weight        Height           BMI      BPSysAve 
##    0.01000000    0.01000000    0.01000000    0.01000000    0.76643012 
## PulseAgeRatio 
##    0.09226396

The model explains the variance in the variables Age, Weight, Height, BMI, and PulseAgeRatio very well. BPSysAve is not well explained judging by the high value of uniqueness.

The loadings of this model are …

fa_result_3$loadings
## 
## Loadings:
##               Factor1 Factor2 Factor3
## Age            0.990                 
## Weight         0.134   0.901   0.405 
## Height                         0.991 
## BMI            0.160   0.977  -0.110 
## BPSysAve       0.454   0.153         
## PulseAgeRatio -0.950                 
## 
##                Factor1 Factor2 Factor3
## SS loadings      2.134   1.806   1.166
## Proportion Var   0.356   0.301   0.194
## Cumulative Var   0.356   0.657   0.851

Factor 1 - explains 35.6% variance - Age has a high loading(0.990) => F1 is strongly associated with age. - BPSysAve has a moderate loading (0.454) => BPSysAve has some association with F1 - PulseAgeRatio has a strong negative loading (-0.950) => as F1 increases, PulseAgeRatio decreases

Interpretation of F1: Factor 1 captures the variables, which are related to age and health indicators that are affected by age, like BPSysAve and PulseAgeRatio. This factor could represent the health of a person related to their age.

Factor 2 - explains 30.1% variance - Weight has a high loading (0.901) => F2 is strongly associated with weight. - BMI has a high loading (0.977), F2 is also strongly associated with body composition and mass.

Interpretation of F2: Factor 2 appears to represent the body mass or body composition of a person based on the high loadings of the Weight and BMI variables.

Factor 3 - explains 19.4% variance - Height has a high loading (0.991) => F3 is strongly associated with height. - Weight has a moderate loading (0.405) => Weight has some association with F3

Interpretation of F3: Factor 3 is probably a “Height” factor that captures the variance in height with minor contribution of weight.

For fa_result_2_f3(best for predicting new data) we have the following uniqueness and loadings

fa_result_2_f3$uniquenesses
##           Age        Weight        Height           BMI      BPSysAve 
##    0.01000000    0.01000000    0.01000000    0.01000000    0.76605269 
##      BPDiaAve PulseAgeRatio 
##    0.94114083    0.09223123

The model explains the variance in the variables Age, Weight, Height, BMI, and PulseAgeRatio very well. BPSysAve and BPDiaAve are not well explained judging by the high value of uniqueness.

fa_result_2_f3$loadings
## 
## Loadings:
##               Factor1 Factor2 Factor3
## Age            0.991                 
## Weight         0.109   0.904   0.404 
## Height                         0.990 
## BMI            0.146   0.979  -0.110 
## BPSysAve       0.450   0.161         
## BPDiaAve       0.118   0.163   0.136 
## PulseAgeRatio -0.949                 
## 
##                Factor1 Factor2 Factor3
## SS loadings      2.134   1.851   1.181
## Proportion Var   0.305   0.264   0.169
## Cumulative Var   0.305   0.569   0.738

Both model appear to have similar factors - Factor 1 is about the health related to the age. Represents 30.5% variance. - Factor 2 represent the body mass or body composition of a person. Represents 26.4% variance - Factor 3 is about the height of the person with a small contribution of the weight. Represents 16.9% variance.

Clustering

Explore in what groups can people be divided based on their health metrics to judge their health risk.

clustering_data_health_risk = final_cleaned_data %>% dplyr::select("BMI","BPSysAve",
                                                                  "BPDiaAve", "TotChol",
                                                                  "Pulse")
ggcorr(clustering_data_health_risk)

heatmap(cor(clustering_data_health_risk),symm=T)

# K means

k = 4
set.seed(1)
fit <- kmeans(clustering_data_health_risk, k, nstart = 25, iter.max = 100)
groups = fit$cluster
barplot(table(groups), col="pink")

fviz_cluster(fit, data = clustering_data_health_risk, geom = c("point"),ellipse.type = 'norm', pointsize=1)+
  theme_minimal()+scale_fill_brewer(palette="Paired")

Based on the kmeans clustering plot we can see that there is quite a lot of overlapping between the different clusters. We can now check the silhouette width to further determine the goodness of the clustering algorithm.

d <- dist(clustering_data_health_risk, method="euclidean")  
sil = silhouette(groups, d)
plot(sil, col=1:5, main="", border=NA)

summary(sil)
## Silhouette of 4372 units in 4 clusters from silhouette.default(x = groups, dist = d) :
##  Cluster sizes and average silhouette widths:
##       993       931       991      1457 
## 0.1418206 0.1723190 0.1229947 0.2103767 
## Individual silhouette widths:
##     Min.  1st Qu.   Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max. 
## -0.04853  0.07736  0.15388  0.16689  0.25103  0.44880

Values are closer to zero which indicates that the clusters may overlap. The negative minimum silhouette width also shows that there are some points that have been miscalculated.

We will now try to find what would be the ideal number of clusters using different approaches.

fviz_nbclust(clustering_data_health_risk, kmeans, method = 'silhouette', k.max = 20, nstart = 25, iter.max = 100)

Here we can see that the optimal number of clusters will be 2.

fviz_nbclust(clustering_data_health_risk, kmeans, method = 'wss', k.max = 20, 
             nstart = 25, iter.max = 100)

Judging by the Elbow Plot, we can assume that 4 or 5 cluster will be enough.

fviz_nbclust(clustering_data_health_risk, kmeans, method = 'gap_stat', k.max = 6, nstart = 15, nboot = 50, iter.max = 50)
## Warning: Quick-TRANSfer stage steps exceeded maximum (= 218600)
## Warning: Quick-TRANSfer stage steps exceeded maximum (= 218600)
## Warning: Quick-TRANSfer stage steps exceeded maximum (= 218600)

# Please ignore any warnings shown. It takes a lot of time to be run.

Based on the Gap Statistic Plot, 2 clusters would be the optimal number for our dataset.

The three graphs give us slightly different answers so we can try and do clustering with 2 centers and with 3 centers and after that decide which is better.

k = 3
fit_3 <- kmeans(clustering_data_health_risk, k, nstart = 25, iter.max = 100)
groups_3 = fit_3$cluster
fviz_cluster(fit_3, data = clustering_data_health_risk, geom = c("point"),ellipse.type = 'norm', pointsize=1)+
  theme_minimal()+scale_fill_brewer(palette="Paired")

centers_3=fit_3$centers
for(i in 1:3){
  b = barplot(centers_3[i,], las=2, col="pink")
}

print(fit_3$size)
## [1] 1256 1718 1398
print(fit_3$centers)  
##           BMI   BPSysAve   BPDiaAve    TotChol      Pulse
## 1  0.38769736  0.1027518  0.4376234  0.1891136  0.7928917
## 2 -0.37634177 -0.5844705 -0.4868050 -0.5688613 -0.2891907
## 3  0.09887191  0.4792305  0.4222648  0.4886598 -0.7513537

Based on the centers we can interpret what each cluster will represent. Cluster 1 - 1256 individuals - BMI: 0.388 — higher-than-average BMI => could mean higher body weight - BPSysAve: 0.103 — close to the average - BPDiaAve: 0.438 — higher than average, but not by a lot - TotChol: 0.189 — slightly above average; could increase cardiovascular risk - Pulse: 0.793 — far above average; the group might be under more physical or metabolic stress, or may worse cardiovascular fitness

Interpretation of C1: This cluster show a group that has higher BMI and pulse rate and close to average blood pressure and cholesterol levels. These individuals could have an average rick of cardiovascular issues.

Cluster 2 - 1718 people: - BMI: -0.376 — people with lower-than-average BMI - BPSysAve: -0.584 — lower than average(but not severly so), which might mean that this group has healthier blood pressure levels - BPDiaAve: -0.487 — lower than average - TotChol: -0.569 — lower cholesterol levels, which is healthier - Pulse: -0.289 — The pulse rate is also slightly lower than average, which along with lower BMI, TotChol and blood pressure could mean better cardiovascular fitness

Interpretation of C2: This cluster shows healthier individuals with below average BMI, blood pressure, cholesterol and pulse. This could indicate that they have a lower risk of cardiovascular issues.

Cluster 3 - 1398 individuals - BMI: 0.099 — close to the average, normal body weight - BPSysAve: 0.479 — higher-than-average, could increase cardiovascular risk - BPDiaAve: 0.422 — higher-than-average - TotChol: 0.489 — higher levels, could increase cardiovascular risk - Pulse: -0.751 — lower than average; could mean better cardiovascular fitness or it could mean that the body has a compensatory response to manage higher blood pressure

Interpretation of C3: This cluster shows individuals with normal BMI, but higher blood pressure and cholesterol levels. They might have a higher risk for cardiovascular conditions and could benefit from changing their diet and contacting a doctor to find a way to regulate their blood pressure.

d <- dist(clustering_data_health_risk, method="euclidean")  
sil = silhouette(groups_3, d)
plot(sil, col=1:5, main="", border=NA)

summary(sil)
## Silhouette of 4372 units in 3 clusters from silhouette.default(x = groups_3, dist = d) :
##  Cluster sizes and average silhouette widths:
##      1256      1718      1398 
## 0.1253348 0.2193853 0.1574696 
## Individual silhouette widths:
##     Min.  1st Qu.   Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max. 
## -0.05336  0.08088  0.16416  0.17257  0.25919  0.45006

Values are close to zero which indicates that the clusters may overlap. The negative minimum silhouette width also shows that there are some points that have been miscalculated.

k = 2
fit_2 <- kmeans(clustering_data_health_risk, k, nstart = 25, iter.max = 100)
groups_2 = fit_2$cluster
fviz_cluster(fit_2, data = clustering_data_health_risk, geom = c("point"),ellipse.type = 'norm', pointsize=1)+
  theme_minimal()+scale_fill_brewer(palette="Paired")

print(fit_2$size)
## [1] 2194 2178
print(fit_2$centers)
##          BMI   BPSysAve   BPDiaAve    TotChol       Pulse
## 1 -0.2958245 -0.5204895 -0.3978986 -0.4754041 -0.21898393
## 2  0.2881794  0.4301442  0.5402387  0.4528953 -0.03255248

Based on the centers we can interpret what each cluster will represent. Cluster 1 - 2194 individuals - BMI: - 0.296 - little below the average - BPSysAve: -0.520 — below average, relatively healthier blood pressure levels. - BPDiaAve: -0.398 — below average. - TotChol: -0.475 — lower; could mean a lower risk of cardiovascular issues. - Pulse: -0.219 — little below average; could mean good cardiovascular health

Interpretation of C1: This cluster shows people that are healthier and have below average BMI, blood pressure, cholesterol levels and pulse. They could have lower risk for cardiovascular issues.

Cluster 2 - 2178 individuals - BMI: 0.288 — higher BMI; could mean greater body weight - BPSysAve: 0.430 — higher than average, could mean higher chance cardiovascular risk - BPDiaAve: 0.540 — above average, supporting the potential for cardiovascular risk - TotChol: 0.453 — higher-than-average; increased risk for heart diseases - Pulse: -0.033 — close to average

Interpretation of C2: This cluster shows people that have higher than average BMI, blood pressure, cholesterol levels, and pulse. This could indicate a higher chance of cardiovascular issues. They would benefit from changing their lifestyle and contacting a doctor.

d <- dist(clustering_data_health_risk, method="euclidean")  
sil = silhouette(groups_2, d)
plot(sil, col=1:5, main="", border=NA)

summary(sil)
## Silhouette of 4372 units in 2 clusters from silhouette.default(x = groups_2, dist = d) :
##  Cluster sizes and average silhouette widths:
##      2194      2178 
## 0.2302622 0.1603961 
## Individual silhouette widths:
##     Min.  1st Qu.   Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max. 
## -0.05632  0.10679  0.19905  0.19546  0.28812  0.42861

Values are again close to zero, but they are better than for 3 clusters. From this we can assume that the clustering with only 2 centers is better than the one with 3.

PAM

fit_pam <- eclust(clustering_data_health_risk, "pam", stand=TRUE, k=4, graph=F)

fviz_cluster(fit_pam, data = clustering_data_health_risk, geom = c("point"), pointsize=1)+
  theme_minimal()+scale_fill_brewer(palette="Paired")

Number of groups by pam:

fviz_nbclust(clustering_data_health_risk, pam, method = 'silhouette', k.max = 10)

# This takes a minute to run!

Based on this graph, the optimal number of clusters is 2.

fit_pam_2 <- eclust(clustering_data_health_risk, "pam", stand=TRUE, k=2, graph=F)
adjustedRandIndex(fit_2$cluster, fit_pam_2$clustering) 
## [1] 0.7916127

The value(0.79) is close to 1, so we can say that the two classifications are in agreement.

sil_pam <- silhouette(fit_pam_2$clustering, dist(clustering_data_health_risk))
summary(sil_pam)
## Silhouette of 4372 units in 2 clusters from silhouette.default(x = fit_pam_2$clustering, dist = dist(clustering_data_health_risk)) :
##  Cluster sizes and average silhouette widths:
##      2149      2223 
## 0.1617745 0.2230706 
## Individual silhouette widths:
##     Min.  1st Qu.   Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max. 
## -0.09266  0.10372  0.19756  0.19294  0.28601  0.42480

Worse silhouette widths than k means.

Kernel k-means

fit_ker <- kkmeans(as.matrix(clustering_data_health_risk), centers=2, kernel="rbfdot")
## Using automatic sigma estimation (sigest) for RBF or laplace kernel
#Also takes a minute to run!
object_ker = list(data = clustering_data_health_risk, cluster = fit_ker@.Data)
fviz_cluster(object_ker, geom = c("point"), ellipse=F,pointsize=1)+
  theme_minimal()+scale_fill_brewer(palette="Paired")

adjustedRandIndex(fit_2$cluster, fit_ker@.Data) 
## [1] 0.6704373

These are less of in agreement compared to the previous ones.

clusters_ker <- fit_ker@.Data
kernel_matrix <- kernelMatrix(rbfdot(), as.matrix(clustering_data_health_risk))
sil_ker <- silhouette(clusters_ker, as.dist(1 - kernel_matrix))
summary(sil_ker)
## Silhouette of 4372 units in 2 clusters from silhouette.default(x = clusters_ker, dist = as.dist(1 - kernel_matrix)) :
##  Cluster sizes and average silhouette widths:
##       2552       1820 
## 0.02599885 0.06525317 
## Individual silhouette widths:
##     Min.  1st Qu.   Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max. 
## -0.04128  0.01410  0.03448  0.04234  0.06197  0.18345

Very low score, indicating bad classification.

EM clustering

res.Mclust <- Mclust(clustering_data_health_risk)
summary(res.Mclust)
## ---------------------------------------------------- 
## Gaussian finite mixture model fitted by EM algorithm 
## ---------------------------------------------------- 
## 
## Mclust VEE (ellipsoidal, equal shape and orientation) model with 7 components: 
## 
##  log-likelihood    n df       BIC      ICL
##       -25997.65 4372 62 -52515.04 -56808.1
## 
## Clustering table:
##    1    2    3    4    5    6    7 
## 1022  828  737  432  540  293  520
fviz_mclust(object = res.Mclust, what = "BIC", pallete = "jco") +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c(1:10))
## Warning: `gather_()` was deprecated in tidyr 1.2.0.
## ℹ Please use `gather()` instead.
## ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the factoextra package.
##   Please report the issue at <https://github.com/kassambara/factoextra/issues>.
## This warning is displayed once every 8 hours.
## Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was
## generated.
## Warning in scale_x_discrete(limits = c(1:10)): Continuous limits supplied to discrete scale.
## ℹ Did you mean `limits = factor(...)` or `scale_*_continuous()`?

The optimal number of cluster is 7 here.

fviz_mclust(object = res.Mclust, what = "classification", geom = "point",
            pallete = "jco")

fit_7 <- kmeans(clustering_data_health_risk, 7, nstart = 25, iter.max = 100)
print(adjustedRandIndex(res.Mclust$classification, fit_7$cluster))
## [1] 0.2492618

As we can see by the low values they are not in agreement.

sil_em <- silhouette(res.Mclust$classification, dist(clustering_data_health_risk))
summary(sil_em)
## Silhouette of 4372 units in 7 clusters from silhouette.default(x = res.Mclust$classification, dist = dist(clustering_data_health_risk)) :
##  Cluster sizes and average silhouette widths:
##        1022         828         737         432         540         293 
## -0.01736362  0.15736536  0.13334305  0.07685776  0.16139390 -0.03916350 
##         520 
##  0.01587654 
## Individual silhouette widths:
##     Min.  1st Qu.   Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max. 
## -0.29279 -0.02462  0.08163  0.07501  0.17703  0.39302

The silhoutte widths of the EM clustering are also very low, indicating bad classification.
